title: Holy Scripture: WCF Chapter 1 (Sections 1-3)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Holy Scripture - WCF Chapter 1 (sections 1-3) - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Starting a Psalm 119 Series with WCF Chapter 1
subpoints: Beginning a series on Psalm 119, which is entirely about the Word of God and the God of the Word; plan to do 23 lessons (8 verses AM, 8 verses PM each Lord's Day)
For the first month of the series, Pastor Lyro will also work through WCF Chapter 1 to give a systematic view of what the Bible says about itself — doctrine alongside the Psalm
Westminster Confession begins with Scripture because everything else we believe flows from what we believe about the Bible (God in ch. 2, creation and providence in later chapters)
The Confession opens: 'Of the Holy Scripture' — a fitting starting point for all Christian doctrine

outline.title: WCF 1.1 — Paragraph 1: Creation Reveals God but Cannot Save
subpoints: 'Although the light of nature and the works of creation and providence do so far manifest the goodness, wisdom, and power of God as to leave man inexcusable, yet are they not sufficient to give that knowledge of God and of his will which is necessary unto salvation'
Psalm 19:1-3: the heavens declare the glory of God; there is no speech or language where their voice is not heard — creation proclaims God to everyone, everywhere, in every age
Romans 1:18-20: 'what may be known of God is manifest in them; his eternal power and Godhead are clearly seen... so that they are without excuse'; man understands what nature says about God

But man suppresses this knowledge (Romans 1:21-25): 'although they knew God, they did not glorify him as God... they exchanged the truth of God for the lie'; Psalm 14 and 53 — the fool continually says 'there is no God' precisely because the knowledge keeps surfacing
Implication: no one at the last day can claim they never heard of God; creation has proclaimed him to every soul — this should drive us to evangelism and missions (Romans 10:14-17)

outline.title: WCF 1.1 (cont.) — Special Revelation and the Sufficiency of Scripture
subpoints: 'Therefore it pleased the Lord at sundry times and in divers manners to reveal himself and to declare his will unto his church' — Hebrews 1:1-2

God spoke in many ways throughout history: directly (to Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Samuel, Jesus), through theophanies, through dreams and visions, through angels, through the Urim and Thummim, through the prophets

'God has in these last days spoken to us by his Son' — the Son is the fulfillment of all those prior modes; as Christ fulfilled the sacrificial system, so he fulfilled all those earlier forms of revelation

'Afterwards for the better preserving and propagating of the truth... to commit the same wholly unto writing' — God gathered all these revelations into Scripture; Scripture was given for the comfort of his people (Romans 15:4) and against the malice of Satan and the world

'Those former ways of God revealing his will being now ceased' — cessationism follows from the completion of the canon in Christ; Ephesians 2:20: apostles and prophets are foundation material, no longer needed once the foundation is laid

outline.title: WCF 1.2 — Paragraph 2: The Canon of Scripture
subpoints: 'Under the name of Holy Scripture, or the word of God written, are now contained all the books of the Old and New Testaments' — lists the 66 books; 'all of which are given by inspiration of God to be the rule of faith and life'
The 39 OT books and 27 NT books — not too many, not too few; all and only these are the Word of God
The word 'contained' means the canon is complete — there is nothing outside Scripture that is also Word of God; it is self-contained and sufficient

outline.title: WCF 1.3 — Paragraph 3: The Apocrypha
subpoints: 'The books commonly called Apocrypha, not being of divine inspiration, are no part of the canon of the Scripture; and therefore are of no authority in the church of God, nor to be any otherwise approved or made use of than other human writings'

The Apocrypha refers specifically to the extra books in the Roman Catholic Bible: Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Ecclesiasticus (Sirach), Baruch, 1-2 Maccabees; plus three additions to Daniel (Susanna, Song of the Three Children, Bel and the Dragon)

Five reasons these books are not canonical: (1) Never part of the Hebrew scriptures — rejected by Jews as Scripture; (2) Christ and the apostles never quote them (though they quote secular Greek philosophers); (3) the early church fathers did not embrace them as a whole; (4) the Roman Catholic Church did not officially accept them until the Council of Trent in 1546 — a post-Reformation counter-measure; (5) the books themselves do not claim divine inspiration and the best of them explicitly disclaim it

The Apocrypha is not pseudopigrapha (falsely attributed) — it is intertestamental literature; 1 Maccabees is a valuable historical source for the period between Malachi and Matthew; the books are not evil, just not Scripture
The doctrine of purgatory depends on 2 Maccabees — without that book, there is no biblical basis for purgatory

summary:
This is the first lesson in a Sunday school series on Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 1, run concurrently with a new sermon series on Psalm 119. The two series are designed to work together — Psalm 119 provides the devotional and exegetical encounter with Scripture's excellence, while the WCF study gives the systematic theological framework for what the Bible says about itself.

The lesson walks through the first three paragraphs of WCF Chapter 1. Paragraph 1 teaches that the light of nature and the works of creation and providence reveal enough of God to leave all people inexcusable — Psalm 19 and Romans 1 are the key texts. Every person in every age and place knows God exists from creation, but suppresses that knowledge. However, general revelation cannot save: salvation requires hearing the Word preached (Romans 10). God therefore revealed himself in special ways throughout redemptive history — directly, through theophanies, visions, dreams, angels, prophets — culminating in his Son (Hebrews 1:1-2). All of these prior modes are now ceased, having been fulfilled in Christ and gathered into written Scripture.

Paragraph 2 names the 66 canonical books as 'given by inspiration of God to be the rule of faith and life.' Paragraph 3 addresses the Apocrypha — the extra books in the Roman Catholic Bible — explaining five reasons they are not canonical: Jewish rejection, the silence of Christ and the apostles, the absence of patristic consensus, the late official Catholic acceptance (Council of Trent, 1546), and the books' own disclaimers of inspiration. The lesson includes lively Q&A from the congregation throughout.
transcript:
## Introduction: Two Series Together

This morning during the morning service we are going to start a series on Psalm 119 — a series I have been wanting to do for a while but have been a little scared of, just because of the sheer size of the Psalm. I was also afraid it could become repetitive. But the more I have studied it, the more I see that it is not repetitive. So we are going to embark on this series.

There will be 23 lessons. We are going to do it a little differently: this will be the text for the morning service, so we will do eight verses in the morning and eight verses in the afternoon service. If you are not here for the afternoon service, the solution is simple: it is the Lord's Day — not the Lord's Hour. You can stay for the afternoon service too.

As we start this series on a Psalm that is all about the Word of God and the God of the Word — that is really the theme of Psalm 119 — I thought it would be profitable for this first month of the series to look at the doctrine of the Word of God from the Word of God in a more systematic way. For between four to six lessons I want to look at Chapter 1 of our Confession and then later the chapter on the Law of God. Those two things going together give us a great understanding of what the Bible says about itself. The Confession is a great place to start, so you will need your Bible and your hymnal — because our hymnal includes our Confession of Faith. If you turn to page 847, the little numbers at the bottom, you will find Chapter 1 of our Confession.

## WCF Chapter 1: "Of the Holy Scripture"

It makes sense that the Confession starts here, because everything else we believe flows from what we believe about the Bible. What you believe about God in Chapter 2, what you believe about creation and providence — all of that flows from the Scriptures. So we confess first what we believe about the Bible.

### Paragraph 1

Let me read Paragraph 1:

*Although the light of nature and the works of creation and providence do so far manifest the goodness, wisdom, and power of God as to leave man inexcusable, yet are they not sufficient to give that knowledge of God and of his will which is necessary unto salvation. Therefore, it pleased the Lord at sundry times and in divers manners to reveal himself and to declare his will unto his church; and afterwards, for the better preserving and propagating of the truth, and for the more sure establishment and comfort of the church against the corruption of the flesh and the malice of Satan and all the world, to commit the same holy unto writing; which maketh the Holy Scripture to be most necessary; those former ways of God's revealing his will unto his people being now ceased.*

What do we see here? Four things.

**First: creation reveals to everyone that there is a God.**

Psalm 19:1-3: *The heavens declare the glory of God; and the firmament shows his handiwork. Day unto day utters speech, and night unto night reveals knowledge. There is no speech nor language where their voice is not heard.*

There has never been a person in the history of the world who did not know that God exists, because creation proclaims it. Paul teaches the same in Romans 1:18-20: *For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who suppress the truth in unrighteousness... For since the creation of the world his invisible attributes are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead, so that they are without excuse.*

There is no complication here. Paul says that creation tells everyone that God exists. And notice: the text says man *understands* what creation is saying about God. He does not say man is confused. He understands. But what does man do with that understanding? He suppresses it. Romans 1:21-25: *Although they knew God, they did not glorify him as God, nor were thankful, but became futile in their thoughts, and their foolish hearts were darkened... they exchanged the truth of God for the lie and worshiped and served the creature rather than the Creator.*

Psalm 14 and 53 both open with: *The fool has said in his heart, "There is no God."* And the way it is written in the original, it means he continually says it — because the moment he stops telling himself there is no God, that knowledge just pops right back up from creation. He has to keep suppressing it.

So what does humanity do? They want to worship, but they do not want to acknowledge the true God. So they worship false gods — they worship the creature rather than the Creator.

Our Confession says that because God reveals himself in creation, man cannot claim he never heard about God. Paul says it plainly: they are without excuse. The practical implication: no one at the last day can say, "But I didn't know there was a God." Creation has proclaimed him — only every star, only every grain of sand, only every cloud in the sky. And we decided not to hear that.

This should drive us to evangelism and missions. Remember what Jesus says: I did not come to condemn the world — the world already stands condemned. So get out and proclaim the gospel.

**Second: general revelation cannot save.**

The Confession says: *yet they are not sufficient to give that knowledge of God and of his will which is necessary unto salvation.* 1 Corinthians 2:14: *The natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him; nor can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned.* 1 Corinthians 1:21: *It pleased God through the foolishness of the message preached to save those who believe.* It is through preaching — not through creation, not through general revelation — that people are saved.

Romans 10:8-17: *How then shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher? And how shall they preach unless they are sent?* Paul is clear: the only way to be saved is through hearing the proclamation of God's Word. The burden is on the church. *How shall they hear if they are not sent?* The responsibility is on our shoulders to send, to go, and to proclaim.

**Third: God revealed himself in a special way besides creation.**

*Therefore it pleased the Lord at sundry times and in divers manners to reveal himself and to declare his will unto his church* — this is the language of Hebrews 1:1: *God, who at various times and in various ways spoke in time past to the fathers by the prophets.*

God spoke in many ways throughout redemptive history: directly to Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Samuel, Jesus; through theophanies — visual appearances of God the Son (the Angel of the LORD appearing to Abraham, Jacob, Gideon, Manoah's parents); through dreams (Joseph, Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar, Daniel, Joseph in the NT); through visions; through angels; through the Urim and Thummim; through prophets (too many to list).

And then the rest of Hebrews 1:1-2: *Has in these last days spoken to us by his Son.* The contrast is important: God used to speak in all these ways, but now he has spoken through his Son — and the Son's Word is the fulfillment of all those other modes. We do not need them anymore, just as we do not need animal sacrifices anymore. They have been fulfilled.

**Fourth: God committed this special revelation to writing.**

*Afterwards for the better preserving and propagating of the truth... to commit the same wholly unto writing, which maketh the Holy Scripture to be most necessary.*

God gave us the Bible for several reasons: for the comfort of his people (Romans 15:4); for preserving and propagating the truth (Luke 1:4 — Luke writes so Theophilus can know the truth); to stand against the malice of Satan and the world (Ephesians 6; remember how Jesus used the Word against Satan in the temptation).

*Those former ways of God's revealing his will unto his people being now ceased.* The diverse ways — trances, visions, prophets, priests, theophanies, angels — have been fulfilled in Jesus Christ and the completed canon. Ephesians 2:20: apostles and prophets are the foundation of the church. You do not need the foundation once it is laid; you need the materials to build the walls and the roof. Once the canon was complete with Revelation, the gifts that were given to confirm the authenticity of the Word are no longer needed — because the canon is established.

### Paragraph 2

Paragraph 2 names the canon: *Under the name of Holy Scripture, or the word of God written, are now contained all the books of the Old and New Testaments...* — here the Confession lists all 66 books — *all of which are given by inspiration of God to be the rule of faith and life.*

The Old Testament has 39 books; the New Testament has 27. Not missing any. Not too many. All and only these are the Word of God.

Notice the word "contained." It means the canon is complete — there is nothing outside Scripture that is also Word of God. It is self-sufficient.

### Paragraph 3

Paragraph 3 addresses the Apocrypha:

*The books commonly called Apocrypha, not being of divine inspiration, are no part of the canon of the Scripture; and therefore are of no authority in the church of God, nor to be any otherwise approved or made use of than other human writings.*

The Roman Catholic Bible is larger than the Protestant Bible, and the Eastern Orthodox Bible is larger still. The word Apocrypha technically refers to the extra books in the Roman Catholic canon: Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Ecclesiasticus (or Sirach), Baruch, 1 and 2 Maccabees — plus three additions to Daniel: the Story of Susanna, the Song of the Three Children, and Bel and the Dragon.

Why are these books not canonical? Five reasons.

1. They were never part of the Hebrew Scriptures. The mainstream of Judaism never recognized them as canonical.
2. Christ and the apostles never quote from them. They quote secular Greek philosophers (Paul in Acts 17 and Titus), but never from the Apocrypha. That is a significant silence.
3. The early church fathers as a whole did not embrace these books as canonical.
4. Even the Roman Catholic Church did not officially accept them until the Council of Trent in 1546 — after the Reformation had already begun. This was a counter-Reformation measure, partly because one of their central doctrines (purgatory) rests on 2 Maccabees, and without 2 Maccabees the biblical case for purgatory collapses.
5. The books themselves do not claim inspiration. The best of them — like the Wisdom of Ben Sirach — actually explicitly disclaim divine inspiration. A document that says "this is not the Word of God" cannot be the Word of God.

This does not mean these are evil books. 1 Maccabees is actually quite a good historical source for the intertestamental period — the 400 years between Malachi and Matthew. They are just not Scripture. They are human writings, worth reading as historical literature, but not binding on the conscience as the Word of God. It would be like citing Jay Bridges' *Trusting God* as a command from God — a good book, helpful, but not the Bible.

### Closing

God has promised to preserve his Word, and he has done so. *The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God stands forever.* He has kept that promise. We have the Bible today because God has been faithful.

*Father, thank you that you are a good God. Thank you that you speak through your Word. Thank you that you have given us a church full of people who are able to understand your Word and rightly state what it teaches. Pray that we would be faithful to your Word. In Jesus' name, amen.*
